title.en: MTEB Leaderboard: Automated Evaluation Pipelines for Embedding Models

description.en: Build automated pipelines that query the MTEB leaderboard programmatically, filter models by task-specific metrics, and run head-to-head evaluations to select the optimal embedding model for your RAG system.

story_variants.en:
## From Manual Browsing to Automated Model Selection

Vaathiyaar here! You've heard of the MTEB leaderboard — the gold standard for comparing embedding models. But are you still scrolling through a web page, eyeballing scores? That's the slow way. In this lesson, you'll build **automated evaluation pipelines** that pull leaderboard data, filter by your constraints, and run local head-to-head benchmarks — all in Python. By the end, you'll have a reusable workflow that answers "which embedding model should I deploy?" with evidence, not guesswork.

### How the MTEB Leaderboard Works

The MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) evaluates models on **8 task categories** with standardized datasets. Each model's results are published as JSON on Hugging Face, making them machine-readable.

| Task Type | What It Measures | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Retrieval | Document ranking quality | nDCG@10 |
| STS | Semantic similarity accuracy | Spearman correlation |
| Classification | Text label prediction | Accuracy |
| Clustering | Group separation quality | V-measure |
| Reranking | Pairwise relevance ordering | MAP |
| PairClassification | Pair relationship detection | AP |
| Summarization | Summary quality correlation | Spearman |
| BitextMining | Cross-lingual alignment | F1 |

### Fetching Leaderboard Data Programmatically

```python
from huggingface_hub import HfApi
import json

api = HfApi()

# Search for MTEB result files on the Hub
models = api.list_models(
    filter="mteb", sort="downloads", direction=-1, limit=20
)

for model in models:
    print(f"{model.id:45s} downloads={model.downloads}")
```

### Building a Model Filter Pipeline

```python
def filter_models(candidates, constraints):
    """Filter embedding models by deployment constraints."""
    filtered = []
    for model in candidates:
        if model["dims"] > constraints.get("max_dims", float("inf")):
            continue
        if model["size_mb"] > constraints.get("max_size_mb", float("inf")):
            continue
        if model["retrieval_score"] < constraints.get("min_retrieval", 0):
            continue
        filtered.append(model)
    return sorted(filtered, key=lambda m: m["retrieval_score"], reverse=True)

candidates = [
    {"name": "bge-large-en-v1.5", "dims": 1024, "size_mb": 1340, "retrieval_score": 0.544},
    {"name": "bge-small-en-v1.5", "dims": 384, "size_mb": 133, "retrieval_score": 0.514},
    {"name": "all-MiniLM-L6-v2", "dims": 384, "size_mb": 80, "retrieval_score": 0.419},
    {"name": "gte-large-en-v1.5", "dims": 1024, "size_mb": 1340, "retrieval_score": 0.572},
    {"name": "e5-small-v2", "dims": 384, "size_mb": 133, "retrieval_score": 0.495},
]

# Edge deployment: small models only
edge_models = filter_models(candidates, {"max_dims": 512, "max_size_mb": 200})
for m in edge_models:
    print(f"{m['name']:25s} score={m['retrieval_score']:.3f}")
```

### Head-to-Head Local Evaluation

```python
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util
import time

def head_to_head(model_names, corpus, queries):
    """Run head-to-head evaluation on your own data."""
    results = []
    for name in model_names:
        model = SentenceTransformer(name)
        t0 = time.time()
        c_emb = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True)
        encode_ms = (time.time() - t0) * 1000

        hits = 0
        rr_sum = 0.0
        for query_text, gold_idx in queries:
            q_emb = model.encode(query_text, normalize_embeddings=True)
            scores = util.cos_sim(q_emb, c_emb)[0]
            ranked = scores.argsort(descending=True).tolist()
            rank = ranked.index(gold_idx) + 1
            if rank == 1:
                hits += 1
            rr_sum += 1.0 / rank

        results.append({
            "model": name,
            "dims": c_emb.shape[1],
            "encode_ms": encode_ms,
            "hit_rate": hits / len(queries),
            "mrr": rr_sum / len(queries),
        })
    return sorted(results, key=lambda r: r["mrr"], reverse=True)

corpus = [
    "RAG retrieves relevant documents before generating answers",
    "Vector databases store and index embedding vectors",
    "Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained models to domain data",
    "Tokenizers convert text into numerical token sequences",
    "MTEB benchmarks evaluate embedding model quality",
]
queries = [
    ("How does retrieval-augmented generation work?", 0),
    ("Where do you store embeddings?", 1),
    ("What does MTEB measure?", 4),
]

for r in head_to_head(["all-MiniLM-L6-v2", "BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5"], corpus, queries):
    print(f"{r['model']:30s} MRR={r['mrr']:.2f} dims={r['dims']}")
```

### When MTEB Scores Don't Tell the Full Story

| MTEB Says | Reality Check |
|---|---|
| High average score | May be weak on YOUR domain |
| Top retrieval nDCG@10 | Tested on academic datasets, not your corpus |
| Model A > Model B | Could reverse at different sequence lengths |
| Low score on Clustering | Might still excel at your specific clustering task |

> **Key Insight:** MTEB scores are your starting shortlist, not your final answer. The winning workflow is: filter the leaderboard by constraints (size, dims, speed), shortlist the top 3-5 models, then run a head-to-head evaluation on YOUR data. Automate this pipeline so every time a new model drops on the leaderboard, you can evaluate it in minutes — not days.

animation_sequence.content: Stop scrolling leaderboard web pages. In this lesson, you'll build automated pipelines that fetch MTEB results programmatically, filter models by your deployment constraints (size, dimensions, latency), and run head-to-head evaluations on your own data — turning model selection from guesswork into a repeatable, data-driven workflow.

steps.explanation: Define a filter function that takes a list of candidate models and a constraints dictionary. This replaces manual leaderboard scrolling with programmatic filtering.
Initialize an empty list for models that pass all constraints. We'll iterate through candidates and apply each filter.
Check embedding dimensions against max_dims. Higher dimensions mean better quality but more storage — at 1M docs, 1024d needs 4GB vs 384d needs 1.5GB.
Check model file size against max_size_mb. Edge deployments might limit to 200MB; server deployments can handle 1GB+.
Check minimum retrieval score. This ensures you only consider models that meet a quality floor — no point deploying a fast model that returns bad results.
Models passing all filters get added to the shortlist. The constraints use dict.get() with infinity/zero defaults so missing constraints don't filter anything out.
Sort survivors by retrieval_score descending. The top result is your best candidate within your constraints — the model that maximizes quality within your limits.

animation_sequence.code:
import math

def cosine_sim(a, b):
    shared = set(a) & set(b)
    dot = sum(a[w] * b[w] for w in shared)
    mag_a = math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in a.values()))
    mag_b = math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in b.values()))
    return dot / (mag_a * mag_b) if mag_a and mag_b else 0.0

def evaluate_pipeline(encode_fn, corpus, queries):
    corpus_vecs = [encode_fn(doc) for doc in corpus]
    hits, rr_sum = 0, 0.0
    for query_text, gold_idx in queries:
        q_vec = encode_fn(query_text)
        sims = [(i, cosine_sim(q_vec, cv)) for i, cv in enumerate(corpus_vecs)]
        ranked = sorted(sims, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        rank = next(i for i, (idx, _) in enumerate(ranked, 1) if idx == gold_idx)
        hits += 1 if rank == 1 else 0
        rr_sum += 1.0 / rank
    return {"hit_rate": hits / len(queries), "mrr": rr_sum / len(queries)}

steps.explanation: A simple cosine similarity for dict-based sparse vectors. In production you'd use numpy/torch dense vectors, but the math is identical.
The core evaluation function. It takes an encode function, a corpus, and queries with gold-standard relevant doc indices — a mini MTEB retrieval task.
Encode the entire corpus once upfront. This mirrors production indexing — you encode documents at ingestion time, not at query time.
For each query, we encode it and compute similarity against all corpus vectors. The gold_idx tells us which document SHOULD be the top result.
Sort by similarity descending to get the ranked result list. This is what a vector database does internally with approximate nearest neighbor search.
Find the 1-indexed rank of the gold document. If rank is 1, it's a hit. MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) weights rank 2 as 0.5, rank 3 as 0.33, etc.
Return hit_rate (top-1 accuracy) and MRR (average reciprocal rank). These two metrics tell you: does the right doc come first, and if not, how far down is it?

animation_sequence.code:
def automated_model_selection(models, corpus, queries, constraints):
    """End-to-end pipeline: filter → evaluate → rank."""
    # Step 1: Filter by constraints
    shortlist = filter_models(models, constraints)
    print(f"Shortlisted {len(shortlist)} of {len(models)} models")

    # Step 2: Evaluate each on your data
    results = []
    for model in shortlist:
        metrics = evaluate_pipeline(model["encode_fn"], corpus, queries)
        results.append({
            "name": model["name"],
            "mrr": metrics["mrr"],
            "hit_rate": metrics["hit_rate"],
            "dims": model["dims"],
            "size_mb": model["size_mb"],
        })

    # Step 3: Rank by MRR
    results.sort(key=lambda r: r["mrr"], reverse=True)
    return results

steps.explanation: This function is the complete automated pipeline — it combines MTEB-style leaderboard filtering with local evaluation in a single call.
Step 1: Apply deployment constraints to cut the candidate list. No point benchmarking a 7B-parameter model if you're deploying to a 2GB container.
Step 2: Run each shortlisted model through your evaluation function with YOUR corpus and queries — not generic benchmarks.
Collect MRR and hit_rate alongside metadata. This gives you a complete picture: quality + deployment feasibility in one report.
Step 3: Sort by MRR descending. The top result is your recommended model — the one that best retrieves relevant documents within your constraints.
Return the full ranked list so you can inspect runner-ups. Sometimes the #2 model is 10x smaller with only 2% lower MRR — worth considering.

items.right: Query results via API or JSON

items.left: Eyeball scores, pick the top name
items.right: Filter by dims, size, and min quality

items.left: Hope it works on your data
Repeat manually when new models drop

animation_sequence.message: You've built an automated embedding model selection pipeline — from leaderboard filtering to local head-to-head evaluation. No more guessing!

instruction.en: Build an `EvalPipeline` class that automates embedding model evaluation. It should accept an `add_model(name, encode_fn, dims, size_mb)` method to register candidate models, a `filter(max_dims, max_size_mb)` method that returns names of models meeting both constraints, and an `evaluate(corpus, queries)` method that runs retrieval evaluation on all registered models and returns results sorted by MRR descending. Each query is a `(query_text, relevant_doc_index)` tuple. Use the provided `cosine_sim` and `bow_encode` functions.

en: In add_model, store the encode_fn, dims, and size_mb in self.models keyed by name.
In filter, iterate self.models and check both dims <= max_dims and size_mb <= max_size_mb. Return sorted names.

In evaluate, for each model encode all corpus docs, then for each query compute cosine_sim against all docs. Sort sims descending to find the rank of the gold doc. hit_rate counts rank==1; MRR averages 1/rank.

practice_challenges.test_code:
p = EvalPipeline()
p.add_model("a", bow_encode, 256, 50)
p.add_model("b", bow_encode, 1024, 500)
f = p.filter(max_dims=512, max_size_mb=100)
assert f == ["a"]
f2 = p.filter(max_dims=2048)
assert sorted(f2) == ["a", "b"]
c = ["hello world", "foo bar"]
q = [("hello", 0), ("foo bar", 1)]
r = p.evaluate(c, q)
assert len(r) == 2
assert r[0]["mrr"] == r[1]["mrr"]
assert 0 <= r[0]["hit_rate"] <= 1
assert 0 < r[0]["mrr"] <= 1

instruction.en: Build a `LeaderboardAnalyzer` class that processes MTEB-style benchmark results. It stores results via `add_score(model, task_type, task_name, score)` and model metadata via `set_info(model, dims, size_mb)`. Implement `top_models(task_type, n=3)` returning the top-n models by average score for that task type, `model_report(model)` returning a dict with average scores per task type, and `recommend(task_type, max_dims, n=3)` that returns the top-n models for a task type filtered by dims <= max_dims.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class LeaderboardAnalyzer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.scores = {}  # model -> [(task_type, task_name, score), ...]
        self.info = {}    # model -> {"dims": int, "size_mb": float}

    def add_score(self, model: str, task_type: str, task_name: str, score: float):
        # Add a benchmark score for a model
        pass

    def set_info(self, model: str, dims: int, size_mb: float):
        # Store model metadata
        pass

    def top_models(self, task_type: str, n: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
        # Return top-n models by avg score for task_type
        # Each: {"model": str, "avg_score": float, "num_tasks": int}
        # Sorted by avg_score descending, limited to n
        pass

    def model_report(self, model: str) -> dict:
        # Return {task_type: avg_score} for all task types this model has results for
        pass

    def recommend(self, task_type: str, max_dims: int, n: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
        # Like top_models but filtered by dims <= max_dims
        # Only include models that have info set
        # Each: {"model": str, "avg_score": float, "dims": int, "size_mb": float}
        pass

# Test:
la = LeaderboardAnalyzer()
la.add_score("bge-small", "Retrieval", "MSMARCO", 0.51)
la.add_score("bge-small", "Retrieval", "NQ", 0.49)
la.add_score("bge-small", "STS", "STS12", 0.82)
la.add_score("bge-large", "Retrieval", "MSMARCO", 0.59)
la.add_score("bge-large", "Retrieval", "NQ", 0.55)
la.add_score("minilm", "Retrieval", "MSMARCO", 0.42)
la.add_score("minilm", "STS", "STS12", 0.78)

la.set_info("bge-small", dims=384, size_mb=133)
la.set_info("bge-large", dims=1024, size_mb=1340)
la.set_info("minilm", dims=384, size_mb=80)

top = la.top_models("Retrieval", n=2)
print(top[0]["model"])
print(f"{top[0]['avg_score']:.2f}")

report = la.model_report("bge-small")
print(f"{report['STS']:.2f}")

recs = la.recommend("Retrieval", max_dims=512, n=2)
print(recs[0]["model"])
print(len(recs))

en: In add_score, use self.scores.setdefault(model, []).append((task_type, task_name, score)) to accumulate results per model.
In top_models, for each model filter their scores by task_type, compute the average, and sort descending. Use list slicing [:n] to limit results.
In recommend, get the top_models results, then filter by models present in self.info with dims <= max_dims. Attach dims and size_mb from self.info.

practice_challenges.test_code:
la2 = LeaderboardAnalyzer()
la2.add_score("m1", "Retrieval", "t1", 0.8)
la2.add_score("m1", "Retrieval", "t2", 0.6)
la2.add_score("m2", "Retrieval", "t1", 0.9)
la2.set_info("m1", 256, 50)
la2.set_info("m2", 1024, 800)
t = la2.top_models("Retrieval")
assert t[0]["model"] == "m2"
assert abs(t[0]["avg_score"] - 0.9) < 0.001
assert t[1]["model"] == "m1"
assert t[1]["num_tasks"] == 2
r = la2.model_report("m1")
assert "Retrieval" in r
assert abs(r["Retrieval"] - 0.7) < 0.001
rec = la2.recommend("Retrieval", max_dims=512)
assert len(rec) == 1
assert rec[0]["model"] == "m1"
rec2 = la2.recommend("Retrieval", max_dims=1024)
assert len(rec2) == 2
assert rec2[0]["model"] == "m2"
